rationale_for_baseline_tier: Severe, uncontrolled ureteric colic with intractable vomiting and inability to tolerate oral analgesia or fluids, in a patient who lives alone. Telehealth cannot deliver the parenteral analgesia, antiemetic and IV rehydration this presentation requires, nor obtain the CT KUB and calcium/PTH needed to characterise the stone/obstruction and the metabolic cause, nor exclude an obstructed infected kidney. That warrants emergency-department assessment within hours (self- or arranged transport — not self-driving, given the pain and vomiting). It is NOT T5: the patient is haemodynamically stable, conscious, afebrile and still passing urine — call-000 features (urosepsis, anuria, hypercalcaemic-crisis neuro/cardiac signs, collapse) are absent now and carry their own escalation edges.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Fever, feeling hot and cold, or shaking chills (rigors)
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Immediately, at any point — do not wait
escalation_edges.rationale: Fever/rigors with an obstructing stone signals an obstructed, infected kidney (urosepsis) — a surgical emergency needing emergency decompression and IV antibiotics.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you develop a fever, feel hot and cold, or start shaking with chills, call 000 for an ambulance straight away and say you have a kidney stone and might have an infection.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Becoming completely unable to pass any urine
escalation_edges.rationale: Complete anuria may indicate obstruction of a single functioning or both kidneys — an emergency requiring urgent decompression.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you completely stop being able to pass any urine, call 000 — this is an emergency.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Worsening or uncontrollable pain, or continued vomiting with inability to keep any fluids down
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Same day, within hours — this is the baseline action now
escalation_edges.rationale: Oral analgesia and hydration are already failing; the patient needs facility-based parenteral analgesia, an antiemetic and IV fluids without delay, and living alone raises the dehydration risk.
escalation_edges.communication_template: Because the pain is this bad and you can't stop being sick, you need to be seen in hospital today. Arrange a lift or call an ambulance if you can't get there safely — don't drive yourself.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: New confusion or drowsiness, marked worsening weakness, or a fast/irregular heartbeat or palpitations
escalation_edges.rationale: These suggest a dangerous rise in calcium (hypercalcaemic crisis), which can cause cardiac arrhythmia and altered consciousness — a medical emergency, made more likely here by vomiting and dehydration.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you become confused or very drowsy, feel much weaker, or your heart starts racing or beating irregularly, call 000 immediately.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Fainting or collapse, or sudden tearing/ripping abdominal or back pain with feeling faint
escalation_edges.rationale: Collapse or a tearing pain with faintness raises the possibility of a catastrophic vascular cause (e.g. a leaking abdominal aortic aneurysm) that can mimic renal colic in an older adult and cannot be excluded remotely.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you faint or collapse, or the pain suddenly changes to a tearing feeling and you feel like you might pass out, call 000 immediately.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Lives alone with no one at home to help, and is vomiting and unable to keep fluids down
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Lower the threshold to attend in person and arrange transport; the patient cannot rely on someone at home to monitor deterioration or help her get to hospital, and dehydration will progress unobserved.
Keep a leaking abdominal aortic aneurysm in mind as a mimic that cannot be excluded by telehealth — a further reason for prompt in-person assessment rather than home management.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: You need to be seen in person today — go to the nearest emergency department or urgent care now for an injection to control the pain, anti-sickness treatment, fluids into a vein, a scan and blood tests. Because the pain and vomiting are severe, don't drive yourself — arrange a lift, or call an ambulance if you can't get there safely.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Attend the nearest emergency department / urgent care the same day; call 000 for an ambulance if you cannot get there safely or if any emergency features appear.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: Emergency departments and 000 operate 24 hours a day — this should not wait for clinic hours.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Take the urgent requests for a non-contrast CT KUB and for serum calcium + intact PTH (with renal function and urinalysis) with you; GP review within 24 hours to review results and arrange urology and endocrinology referral.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treating the presentation as routine or prompt GP care (or self-care) despite intractable vomiting, uncontrolled pain and a possibly obstructed kidney that cannot be managed or excluded by telehealth.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Untreated severe pain and progressive dehydration/AKI in a patient living alone; an obstructed infected kidney or hypercalcaemic crisis develops unmonitored and becomes life-threatening.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Same-day in-person urgent care is acceptable (minimum viable) only if it can actually deliver parenteral analgesia, an antiemetic, rehydration and urgent imaging/bloods; routing to a facility that cannot leaves the patient under-treated.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Calling a 000 ambulance for a haemodynamically stable, afebrile patient who is still passing urine over-uses emergency resources — 000 is reserved here for the specific red-flag features (urosepsis, anuria, hypercalcaemic-crisis signs, collapse).

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Telehealth cannot confirm the stone or degree of obstruction, cannot exclude an obstructed infected kidney, and cannot measure the anticipated hypercalcaemia / PTH abnormality.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Escalate for in-person parenteral treatment, imaging and bloods; treat the constellation as an underlying metabolic disorder pending confirmation; safety-net explicitly for the time-critical complications.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Anchoring on 'just a kidney stone', reassuring and managing at home, advising more OTC NSAIDs, or asserting/fabricating a calcium or PTH value that has not been measured.
